description:
# balls — architecture

Consolidated spec (2026-06-02): the single current source of truth. Supersedes the prior body
and the 13 working notes it was assembled from. Topic balls under epic bl-b465 track what remains
open; each open topic, when settled, edits the relevant § here directly (no more append-only notes).

## §0 Overview & core principles

balls is a git-native task tracker. All state lives on ONE branch (default `balls`, config/CLI
overridable) of a git repo; persistence is git, local-first. Base balls is the smallest possible
thing — it creates a local `balls` branch and commits task-file changes to it. Everything that
touches the world beyond that branch is a **plugin**. The shipped `tracker` plugin is the only
thing that talks to a remote; the shipped delivery/worktree plugin is the only thing that touches
the project's code. Strip every plugin and balls is a pure local task list.

Load-bearing principles (each enforced structurally, not by discipline):

- **One branch.** Task state never lands on `main` or any other branch. "Nothing on main" is
  STRUCTURAL: base balls never opens the project repo, so core *cannot* leave a commit there.
- **No workflow enforcement in core.** `state` is an open string. The ONLY hardcoded rejection is
  `claim` refusing an already-`claimed` ball. Every other sequence rule (dependencies, gates,
  "review before close") is a plugin pre-hook rejection (best-effort) or skill hinting — never a
  core guarantee. Destination semantics, not source.
- **Plugins are a sequence of binaries.** Invoked blocking, in order, run-parts style, per op-phase.
  Non-zero exit aborts the op and rolls prior plugins back in reverse. That is the whole protocol.
- **Core knows two things about a plugin:** its name and its binary path. It never reads a plugin's
  config. Plugins coordinate only through core schema (task fields), never by sniffing each other.
- **Subtract before adding.** A new verb/state/field/flag is a smell; prefer an existing signal.
  Derive values rather than store them; make a component indifferent rather than teach it cases.

**Vocabulary** (federation/trail theme; see §12): the **anchor** is the always-present,
path-derived local `balls` branch holding this checkout's config + trail pointer. A **trail** is
anchor → step(s) → **terminus**, walked by following each branch's next-step pointer with
stop-on-revisit. **operating** is the local checkout ops physically run against; it resolves to the
terminus. In stealth, anchor == terminus == operating.

## §1 XDG layout

```
$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/balls/
  config.yaml                          # user-level config

$XDG_STATE_HOME/balls/
  plugins/<name>/<plugin-territory>/   # each plugin owns one subtree
    tracker/<pct-enc-remote>/          #   tracker: a clone of the balls branch (.git/ tasks/ config/)
    <delivery>/<pct-enc-local>/<id>/   #   delivery: the code worktree for task <id> (see §11)

  clones/<pct-enc-local-path>/         # per-invocation-path binding
    binding.yaml                       #   which tracker remote (if any) + invocation_path
    operating/                         #   the git repo balls operates against
                                       #     tracker enabled: symlink → plugins/tracker/<remote>/
                                       #     stealth:          a real dir (the anchor itself)
    changes/<uuid>/                    #   in-flight CHANGE worktrees (core, ephemeral, one per op)
    logs/<name>/plugin.log             #   suggested; plugins do as they please
```

- URLs and paths are **percent-encoded, never hashed**, so directory names stay inspectable.
- The CHANGE worktree (`changes/<uuid>/`) is core, off `operating/`, uuid-named (nothing keys off
  the uuid). It is distinct from the delivery plugin's CODE worktree (§11), which lives in plugin
  territory and checks out the *project* repo.
- Two clones sharing one remote URL share one `plugins/tracker/<remote>/` dir.

## §2 Branch layout (committed to `balls`)

```
tasks/<id>.md                          # one file per task; <id> IS the filename basename
config/balls.yaml                      # federated balls config (§4)
config/plugins/<name>/...              # each plugin's federated config; balls never reads it
config/plugins/<op>/<phase>/NN-<name>  # the plugin registry: relative symlinks (§6)
config/plugins/bin/<name>              # local, gitignored: absolute symlink to this box's binary
```

No archive directory. Closed/dropped balls **delete** their `tasks/<id>.md`; history lives in
`git log` (`git log --diff-filter=D -- tasks/<id>.md`; `bl show <id>` reconstructs from history).

## §3 Task schema (one node type)

There is exactly one node type: **task**. `epic`/`issue`/`gate`/`bug` are *tags* with zero core
behavior; any "epic" rendering is emergent from the task having children (§10).

`tasks/<id>.md`:

```markdown
---
title: Refactor the foo system
state: open
created: 2026-05-27T14:32:00Z
updated: 2026-05-28T09:15:00Z
claimant: orionriver@gmail.com
parent: bl-1000
blockers:
  - { id: bl-1100, on: claim }       # I can't be CLAIMED until bl-1100 resolves (dependency)
  - { id: bl-1200, on: close }       # I can't be CLOSED until bl-1200 resolves (gate)
tags: [refactor, infra]
---

Free-form markdown body.
```

- **No `id:` field.** The id is the filename basename of `tasks/<id>.md`, the sole source of truth
  (Model A — "id IS the path, not a field"). One token; `git log -- tasks/<id>.md` and `tasks/`
  greps work by id. (Rejected Model B — opaque-uuid filename + id field — because it forces an
  index for `bl show <id>`; never introduce an index unless it is the core basis of the app.)
- **`state`** is an open string (default `open`). Core validates no transition matrix.
- **`parent`** — containment/tree pointer only; scanned for display (`bl show` tree), never for
  enforcement. Arbitrary depth.
- **`blockers: [{id, on}]`** — the relational primitive (§10), stored on the BLOCKED task. `on` ∈
  `{claim, close}`: which of *this* task's own transitions is gated. The `.pre` is implicit
  (blocking is always a pre-op rejection). Subsumes the old `deps`/`gates` link types — they were
  one edge parameterized by `on`. Pure-metadata links (`relates_to`, `supersedes`, `duplicates`,
  `replies_to`) remain core metadata with no enforcement.
- `created`/`updated` are RFC3339 UTC; `claimant`, `tags` optional. Unknown keys preserved on
  writeback. Terminal ops delete the file (§9).

## §4 Config schema

**Layers, innermost wins** (config layers DOWN the trail; tasks do NOT federate — only config):
1. CLI flags
2. `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/balls/config.yaml`
3. `config/balls.yaml` on this checkout's anchor
4. `config/balls.yaml` on each downstream trail step (§12)
5. Built-in defaults

**Merge semantics:** scalar/object fields — innermost layer fully replaces outer. List fields —
bare `<field>` = full replacement; compose with `<field>_prepend` / `<field>_append` /
`<field>_ban`. The run-parts `config/plugins/<op>/<phase>/` dirs are resolved per-directory up the
trail: the first layer that defines a dir wins WHOLE, no cross-layer merge (deliberately unlike
list fields). Empty dir = run nothing.

**Built-in fields:**
- `branch` (string, default `"balls"`)
- `id_scheme` (object, default `{ prefix: "bl-", length: 4, alphabet: "0123456789abcdef" }`)
- `default_state` (string, default `"open"`)
- `states` (list, default `[open, claimed]`) — informational; terminals (`close`/`drop`) are ops,
  not states; `review` is NOT a state (the verb was dropped, §9). Per-op default-state config is an
  open residual (bl-4778).

There is **no `plugins` config list** — the filesystem symlink registry (§6) IS the plugin chain.

## §5 Commit-message protocol

Every change-attempt commit is `subject / body / trailer-block`, where the trailer block is a
**standard git trailer paragraph** (last blank-line-separated paragraph, `key: value` lines,
parsed via `git interpret-trailers --parse` — no hand-rolled parser; coexists with
`Co-Authored-By:`):

```
<subject — defaults to the ball's title; -m overrides>

<free-form body, optional>

bl-protocol: 1
bl-op: close
bl-id: bl-1234
bl-actor: orionriver@gmail.com
bl-from-state: claimed
bl-to-state: closed
```

- Tokens are lower-kebab (`[a-z0-9-]`, git's trailer grammar). The subject is the ball title so
  `git log` readers never see balls-flavored subjects.
- **Namespacing:** every key is namespaced by owner. `bl-` is RESERVED to core (plugins may not
  emit `bl-*`); plugins prefix with their own name (`jira-id`, `github-url`).
- balls always writes `bl-protocol`, `bl-op`, `bl-actor`; `bl-id` on every op except `init`/`config`;
  `bl-from-state`/`bl-to-state` on state transitions only.
- Repeated key → list (git-native; no comma-splitting). Unknown keys are never dropped — parsed
  into `metadata` and forwarded to plugins on the invoke wire (§7).

## §6 Plugin contract & dispatch

A plugin is a single binary, dispatched **subprocess-uniform** — no in-process path, no privileged
plugins. The shipped `tracker` and delivery plugins are fully separate binaries, in-repo only as
default capabilities + reference implementations, invoked identically to any third party. `bl
plugin <name> <op> <phase>` is the canonical dispatch and balls dogfoods it.

```
<bin> protocol
  → stdout (JSON): { protocol: <version(s)>, ops: [...] }   # self-description; balls never persists it
  → exit 0

<bin> <op> <phase>
  cwd:    the CHANGE worktree (mutating ops) or operating/ (reads)
  env:    BALLS_PROTOCOL=1, BALLS_PLUGIN_NAME=<name>, BALLS_PLUGIN_DEPTH=<n>
  stdin:  payload (§7)
  stdout: diagnostics only — NOTHING is parsed back into state (no return channel; see §7)
  stderr: human log; captured to clones/<enc>/logs/<name>/
  exit:   0 = ok; non-zero = abort + roll prior plugins back in reverse
```

**The registry is the filesystem.** Symlinks under `config/plugins/<op>/<phase>/` on the active
balls branch are the single source of truth for wiring: symlink present = run this plugin in this
op-phase; numeric prefix `NN-` = run order. Two-level stitch for portability:
- COMMITTED (travels with the branch): `<op>/<phase>/NN-<name>` is a RELATIVE symlink → `../bin/<name>`.
  Relative + internal to the checkout, so it is valid in both stealth and federation regardless of
  where the checkout physically sits.
- LOCAL (never committed, gitignored): `config/plugins/bin/<name>` is an ABSOLUTE symlink → this
  machine's binary. A dangling `bin/<name>` = a clean "plugin referenced but not installed here"
  error.

`bl plugin install <name> <path> <ops...>` refreshes `bin/<name>` locally and commits the
`<op>/<phase>/NN-<name>` links (validated against the live `<plugin> protocol` — refuses to link an
op or protocol version the binary doesn't declare). Install is itself an op (commits atomically).

- **Recursion guard:** `BALLS_PLUGIN_DEPTH` (built-in cap). At the cap, nested ops run PLUGIN-FREE
  (suppressed, not refused) — a plugin can always shell back to `bl` without cascading chains. A
  plugin may deliberately re-enable plugins on a nested call.
- **Snapshot:** an op reads the committed `<op>/<phase>/` set at op-start and uses that frozen set;
  an install landing mid-op affects only the next op.
- **Reads are not special-cased.** Every op (incl. `show`/`list`) has hook dirs; reads stay
  plugin-free in PRACTICE only because nothing is linked into them by default.

## §7 Plugin wire payloads

Plugins get meaning directly on the wire — content + intent, not hashes to reverse-engineer.
**There is no return channel:** a plugin contributes by EDITING THE CHANGE WORKTREE (the ball file,
frontmatter, a `git mv`), never by printing values for balls to merge. stdout is diagnostics. Two
plugins writing the same field is two filesystem writes — last writer wins, where "last" is the
run-parts `NN-` order; balls neither arbitrates nor tracks ownership.

**pre payload (stdin):** `protocol`, `op`/`phase`, `plugin_name`, `actor`, `binding`
(`{ remote, branch, operating, invocation_path }` — `invocation_path` is where `bl` was invoked,
the project-repo root the delivery plugin needs, §11), `command` (`op` + intended `field_changes` +
`body_change`), `current_state` (`null` on create). The id is NOT on the pre wire (it is not sealed
yet — a reassigning plugin reads it from the single staged `tasks/*.md`).

**post payload (stdin):** same plus `commit`/`previous_commit`, the final `command`, `metadata`
(parsed from the §5 trailer block, incl. the now-sealed `bl-id`), and `previous_state`/`current_state`
(`null` for create / close-drop respectively).

**rollback payload:** the shape of the op being undone plus `rolling_back: pre|post`; the plugin
tracks its own intermediate state (§11 rollback, general rule in bl-b0b6).

## §8 Op lifecycle

Every op instantiates the same worktree-first shape. The pre/post divider is the COMMIT boundary:
**pre** can still shape what gets committed (the record isn't sealed); **post** reacts to the
sealed commit. The canonical sequence (verb-agnostic):

1. **balls makes the place to work** — the CHANGE worktree on the terminus branch. (For `init`, it
   makes the anchor repo instead — the substrate-creation phase; `init` is a normal op, not a
   dispatch exception.)
2. **balls signals each `pre` plugin in order** — the §7 wire: current state + what it plans to do.
3. **plugins modify the environment** — by editing the worktree (e.g. a create/pre plugin renames
   the ball file to assign an id).
4. **balls commits** the worktree with the §5 trailer block, re-reading the tree to learn the final
   id/state. *This commit is the pre/post boundary.*
5. **balls signals each `post` plugin in order** — what happened, with the sealed `bl-id`/state.
6. **plugins react** — tracker pushes; the delivery plugin acts on the project repo.
7. **integrate + teardown** — balls merges the change worktree into `operating/` and tears it down.
8. **failure / rollback** — any non-zero `pre`/`post` exit rolls prior plugins back in REVERSE order
   FIRST, then discards the change worktree. The discard auto-undoes the worktree's file changes; a
   plugin's external or project-repo side effects are its own `rollback` op's job.

(Mapped to the older five-phase labels: A = step 1, B = 2–3, C = 4, D = 5–6, E = 7.) This sequence
is a draft destined for the final `docs/`.

## §9 Verbs

Deliverable lifecycle verbs: **`create` (`bl new`), `claim`, `unclaim`, `close`, `drop`.** There is
**no `review` verb** — see "close" below.

**`create`** (op `create`; no prior state): balls generates a default-scheme id (§ id generation)
and stages `tasks/<id>.md` (title, `state: default_state`, timestamps, optional `parent`/`tags`,
and any `blockers` the front-door flags imply — §10). A `create/pre` plugin may reject or *reassign*
the id by `git mv`-ing the single staged `tasks/*.md` (it discovers the current name by reading
that file, never hardcoding — so reassigns compose under `NN-` order). balls validates (exactly one
new `tasks/*.md`; regex-valid; no collision) and commits.

**`claim`** (open → claimed; the one hardcoded guard refuses an already-claimed ball): stage
`state: claimed`, `claimant`, bump `updated`. `claim.pre`: the gating plugin rejects if any
claim-blocker is open (§10). `claim.post`: the delivery plugin materializes the code worktree (§11);
balls prints its derived path.

**`unclaim`** (claimed → open) / **`drop`** (terminal): release the claim. `*.post`: the delivery
plugin releases the code worktree. `drop`/`close` stage a DELETION of `tasks/<id>.md`.

**`close`** (claimed → closed) — **delivery and retirement, split across the commit boundary; this
is what `review` used to gesture at.** A task's deliverable life is claim → write code → DELIVER →
RETIRE; in the self-merge default DELIVER and RETIRE are one act, so they are the two halves of
close:
- `close.pre`: (1) the gating plugin rejects if any close-blocker is open (§10); (2) the delivery
  plugin DELIVERS — squashes `work/<id>` → integration (direct variant), conflicts surfacing HERE.
  PRE placement is abort-safe: a failed gate or merge aborts BEFORE the ball-deletion commits, so
  the task stays alive. The delivery/squash plugin sorts LAST (`NN-`) so its un-squash rollback is
  the rare path.
- core commits the `tasks/<id>.md` deletion (`bl-op: close`).
- `close.post`: the delivery plugin tears down the code worktree (§11); the tracker pushes the
  balls-state deletion commit (NEVER the project code branch).

Core close ships no code, pushes no project remote, runs no source-state check. "Close is related
to remotes" = the tracker pushes the *balls* branch, nothing more. `drop` is identical mechanics,
differing only in intent + `bl-to-state: dropped`.

## §10 Blocker & gating model

The one relational primitive is the **blocker** (§3): a `{id, on}` edge on the BLOCKED task naming
which of its own transitions is gated. `on: claim` and `on: close` are the only two use-cases, and
both have direct front doors; the primitive is general (any op) but only these are sugar-supported.

- **dependency** = a claim-blocker ("can't START until it resolves").
- **gate** = a close-blocker ("can't FINISH until it resolves"). Build/test/approval/forge are all
  this.
- **epic** = a task whose children are its claim-blockers — emergent, not a type. It isn't `ready`
  while subtasks are open; when they close it becomes ready (claim it to do closeout, or a plugin
  auto-closes — auto-close is plugin, default off).

**ready(A)** = A is open + unclaimed + every CLAIM-blocker resolved. (A gate child is an open child
that does NOT affect readiness — it's a close-blocker.) **closeable(A)** = every CLOSE-blocker
resolved; checked at `close.pre`. "Resolved" = the blocker is closed OR dropped.

**Deadlock avoidance:** wire exactly ONE edge — a gate child close-blocks its parent; the parent
does NOT claim-block the gate child. So a gate is freely claimable; the reciprocal edge that would
deadlock is simply never created. The gate-check runs against the parent's pre-delivery WORK BRANCH
(which exists once the parent is claimed), so nothing needs the parent formally "done" — no `review`
window is resurrected. "Do the gate after the parent's work" is a skill habit, deliberately
UNENFORCED (enforcing it is the deadlock). Circular blockers are not gated by code (links are
mutable — unlink to fix); `bl doctor` (bl-e8a5) may surface them.

**Enforcement split:**
- CORE stores the schema (`parent`, `blockers`, `state`, `tags`) and ENFORCES NOTHING — it may READ
  blockers to answer `ready`/`closeable` as a hint, but a blocked task is still claimable/closeable
  in naked core.
- The default **gating plugin** is the SOLE enforcer: `claim.pre` rejects on an open claim-blocker,
  `close.pre` on an open close-blocker. Tiny; contributes no CLI.

**Front-door flags are CORE** (they write core schema; plugins are hook binaries and do not extend
`bl`'s parser, so there is no plugin-injected-flag composition). They write the `blockers` edge on
whichever task is blocked; naked core writes the edges but leaves them unenforced without the gating
plugin:
- `--parent E`  → child `parent: E` + `E.blockers += {child, claim}`   (subtask)
- `--gates X`   → child `parent: X` + `X.blockers += {child, close}`   (gate)
- `--needs B`   → `self.blockers += {B, claim}`                         (cross-tree dependency)

**Gates are tasks only** — every gate-check is "is task X closed?". Build/test = a build-gate child
a build plugin creates (`create.post`) and closes on pass; forge gate = a child a forge `sync`
closes on merge; approval = a child a human closes. The resolution mechanism is pluggable; the
blocking mechanism is one thing.

(Open detail: a subtask claim-blocks its parent, leaving a rare gap — add a subtask to an
already-claimed epic, then close it with the child open. Leaning skill-hint "don't" over a second
close-block edge.)

## §11 Delivery / worktree plugin

A SIBLING of tracker (default-wired, separate — so worktrees-without-remote ⊥ remote-without-
worktrees). It owns the deliverable CODE worktree — a `git worktree` of the PROJECT repo on branch
`work/<id>` — end to end. Base balls never opens the project repo, so "nothing on main / nothing in
the project tree" is structural.

**Kind-blind.** The plugin NEVER branches on task kind. It materializes a worktree on `claim.post`,
delivers-if-changes + tears down on `close`, blind to whether the task is an epic, a gate, or real
work. Non-deliverables are normally closed without being claimed → no worktree is ever made; a
claimed non-deliverable gets a harmless EMPTY worktree (the close.pre squash is a no-op when
`work/<id>` has no changes; close.post remove is a no-op when the path is absent). This dissolves
any cross-plugin tag coordination — the worktree plugin reads none of the gating plugin's tags.

**Derived path; stateless across ops.** The worktree path and branch are pure functions of
`(binding, id)`:
```
worktree_path(binding, id) = $XDG_STATE_HOME/balls/plugins/<name>/<pct-enc(binding.invocation_path)>/<id>/
branch                     = work/<id>
```
`<id>` rides the post wire (the immutable `bl-id` trailer), so the plugin RECOMPUTES its resource
each op and checks the filesystem — it needs no id-keyed scratch. Every hook is idempotent by
construction. balls computes+prints the path (preserving "claim prints the path"); the plugin
creates it at the same location — no return channel. The worktree lives in plugin XDG territory,
not the project tree.

**Hooks:** `claim.post` materialize (create-if-absent); `unclaim.post`/`drop.post` release
(remove-if-present); **`close.pre` deliver** (sorts last); **`close.post` teardown** (+ the
best-effort cwd-safety guard: refuse teardown if cwd is inside the worktree — this is where the
"never close from inside the worktree" rule lives, as a plugin precondition, not a core guarantee).

**Two variants** (only "what's wired into the delivery hooks" differs):
- DIRECT (default, local-squash): `close.pre` squashes `work/<id>` → integration as one commit
  whose subject carries the `[bl-id]` delivery tag (the plugin's analog of the §5 trailer; this tag
  is delivery ground truth). Integration branch = `task.target_branch ?? HEAD@project-repo`.
- FORGE-GATED (opt-in): on the agent's intent to finish, the forge bundle pushes `work/<id>`, opens
  a PR, and creates a gate child (§10) blocking close; a forge `sync` closes the child on merge; the
  forge produces the squash, so close.post does NOT squash locally. The gate is enforced by the
  bundle's own `close.pre` check (greenfield core has no gates check to lean on).

**`delivered_in` is a derived query, not a field** — "delivery IS the tag, not a field." The plugin
answers "where was `<id>` delivered?" by tag-scanning (`git log --grep [bl-id]`) the integration
branch; no stored hint, no write/null asymmetry, no staleness. (A cross-clone miss is reported
honestly or resolved by a tracker fetch.)

**Rollback** (specifics; general rule in bl-b0b6): the plugin adds a third side-effect tier (a
project-repo commit not undone by discarding the change worktree). `rollback claim.post` = remove
the worktree + delete `work/<id>`; `rollback close.pre` (direct) = `git reset --hard HEAD~1`
un-squash (always reversible — nothing pushed); (forge) = best-effort delete the remote branch +
drop the gate child. A plugin with an un-undoable side effect sorts LAST in its phase.

## §12 bl init & the federation trail

Core only (a) creates the anchor and (b) runs the configured plugin chain, then commits — it has
zero knowledge of tracker/remotes/stealth. `init` maps onto the normal op shape: phase A creates
the anchor substrate (`git init`, orphan `balls`, seed `config/` + empty `tasks/`, one commit —
never seed empty `<op>/<phase>/` dirs; empty = "run nothing"); the chain's `init/pre`/`init/post`
run the default plugin (tracker), laid by `bl plugin install`'s symlinks (`--plugins <set>`
overrides which; `--plugins []` installs none).

**Tracker's init:** `--stealth` → no-op remote + write a self-reference lock; else resolve a remote
(`--remote` > `--center` > XDG > `origin`), inspect its `balls` branch (absent → create+push; 1
commit → idempotent pass; ≥2 → adopt-via-`bl prime`, not init), and write the next-step pointer +
its run-parts symlinks. **Converging predicate:** `operating/` ends up a valid git repo on `balls`
whether the path was fresh-stealth, freshly-tracked, or re-init — base balls cannot tell which ran.

**Two stealth forms:** unconfigured terminus (`--plugins []` / tracker removed — no pointer, not
locked) vs locked stealth (`--stealth` — tracker no-ops the remote + writes the lock; an explicit
`--remote`/`--center` overrides the lock, no separate flag).

**Trail (bl-62bc):** the next-step pointer (tracker territory: `config/plugins/tracker/remote.yaml`
→ a single scalar `next: <url>`, committed + portable; core never reads it) makes a trail anchor →
step(s) → terminus, walked stop-on-revisit, no depth cap. Two multi-clone models DO NOT conflate:
SHARED HUB (`--remote`/`master_url` — one branch mirrored to N clones, same tasks, NO trail step)
vs FEDERATION (`--center` — your own anchor branch SHADOWS a center as a down-trail layer). The
pointer schema is one scalar — no `branch` field (use the resolved `branch` config, or a
`<url>#<branch>` fragment), no auth hints (git's credential machinery owns auth), no ttl (refresh is
sync's job). Each step gets a parallel checkout at `plugins/tracker/<pct-enc(url)>/` (§1); `bl sync`
re-walks the trail and fast-forwards EVERY step (terminus + intermediates) — the refresh unit is the
whole trail per sync, not per-step timers. `--center` writes the pointer at init; after init, edit
the committed pointer + `bl sync` (no separate "federate" verb).

**Config layering down the trail (bl-62bc):** `config/balls.yaml` layers per §4 innermost-wins,
anchor(innermost) → step → terminus(outermost) — "shadow theirs underneath yours" — no depth cap
(supersedes §4's old "one hop"). Run-parts `<op>/<phase>/` dirs resolve first-defining-layer-wins
WHOLE (no merge); the two rules coexist (value-merge vs identity-select) and N>2 is just the
length-N generalization of §4's two-layer case. The onward pointer itself is per-branch, not
layered. Tasks are TERMINUS-LOCAL — exactly ONE task store on a trail (the terminus's `tasks/`);
steps + anchor contribute config only. That asymmetry is what makes federation "shadow config, not
merge task lists." SEAM: core walks the trail + merges config over LOCAL checkouts (knows nothing of
remotes — stealth = trail length 1, identical code); tracker translates remote↔local (fetches each
`next:` URL into the §1 layout, points `operating/` → terminus). DEFERRED: physical
anchor-vs-operating realization → §1; sync contention + whether `prime` calls `sync` → bl-375a.

Error/notice catalog (verbatim, ownership in brackets): E1 [tracker] no balls remote resolved; E2
[balls] already initialized (use `--reinit`); E3 [tracker] remote already has an established balls
branch (use `bl prime`); E4 [tracker] remote unreachable (refusing to init); E7 [balls] plugin
failed during init, rolled back K prior; W1 [tracker] anchor locked to stealth, not auto-extending;
N3 [balls] downstream layer introduces uninitialized plugins, deferring to next `bl prime`.

## § id generation

`id_scheme = { prefix, length, alphabet }` only (no generator enum). Default
`{ "bl-", 4, "0123456789abcdef" }` — today's scheme. Base balls ships ONE generator (random);
non-default generation (timestamp/sequential/uuid) is a `create/pre` plugin via the same `git mv`
reassign seam — "custom generation" and "plugin-assigned id" are one seam. **Validation is
string-safety**, not an arbitrary charset: `^[A-Za-z0-9][A-Za-z0-9_-]*$` (no `/`, no `.`, no
whitespace/metacharacters, no leading `-`). The default alphabet is lowercase to sidestep
case-insensitive-FS collisions. **Collision:** auto-gen → retry (bounded); plugin-assigned → abort
(an explicit choice is authoritative). id is IMMUTABLE after create; reassignment is a create-only
capability, so during claim/close it rides the wire with no skew.

## §13 Open topics (epic bl-b465)

Each becomes a § edit here when settled.

- **bl-375a** `bl sync` / `bl prime` walkthroughs.
- **bl-b0b6** Pre/post hook rollback — the general rule (external-side-effect tiers, the scratch
  convention for plugins whose state isn't derivable; §11 is the worked derived-state example).
- **bl-e8a5** `bl doctor` — drift checks, stale worktree cleanup, circular-blocker detection.
- **bl-0601** tracker adopt/disown verbs.
- **bl-4778** states-as-config residual: is `claim`'s target state per-op configurable; `bl new
  --state` vs `default_state`. (The settled part — states are open strings, defaults `[open,
  claimed]`, no `review` — is folded into §3/§4/§9.)
- **bl-cef0** migration from current balls (depends on the final shape).
```
